text: The attention mechanism in transformers is genuinely one of the most elegant ideas in deep learning. Instead of compressing the entire input into a fixed vector, you let the model dynamically decide what to focus on at each step. The query, key, value formulation makes a lot of intuitive sense once you think of it like a soft database lookup.

One thing people underestimate about pretraining data quality is how much deduplication matters. If your model sees the same Wikipedia article 500 times, it effectively memorises it rather than learning generalisable patterns. MinHash LSH is worth reading up on if you are building a pipeline for this. The Pile paper has a good discussion on their dedup strategy.

Python generators are one of those features that seem confusing at first but once it clicks you wonder how you ever wrote code without them. The key insight is that a generator function is lazy — it produces values one at a time and only when asked. This makes it perfect for processing large datasets that would not fit in memory all at once.

A recurring mistake in data pipelines is conflating cleaning with filtering. Cleaning means transforming the data to a standard form — lowercasing, stripping HTML, normalising whitespace. Filtering means deciding what to keep versus discard. Separating these into distinct stages makes the pipeline much easier to audit and debug, because you can inspect the intermediate state between each step.
